name: MacTutor
biography:
Heinrich Schr횄쨋ter 's father was a respected merchant who became head of the College of Commerce in K횄쨋nigsberg. Heinrich was the eldest of his parents three sons. His primary education was at a private school then, when he was eleven years old, he entered the Altst횄짚dtischen Gymnasium in K횄쨋nigsberg. There he proved himself a diligent and conscientious pupil and, from the third form onwards, he began reading mathematics books. He was fortunate to have an outstanding teacher of mathematics, Professor M횄쩌ttrich, several of whose pupils went on to become leading mathematicians. Schr횄쨋ter quickly learnt the foundations of the differential calculus and he decided that he wanted to apply mathematics. With expertise in technical drawing and constructing models, he thought that perhaps an occupation as a surveyor would suit him. He graduated from the Gymnasium in 1845 but, inspired by M횄쩌ttrich, quickly changed his mind about his future profession and decided to study mathematics and physics at university. In fact Schr횄쨋ter was the first of a number outstanding mathematics pupils at the K횄쨋nigsberg Gymnasium, all influenced by M횄쩌ttrich; Rudolf Lipschitz , Carl Neumann , and Alfred Clebsch were three to four years behind Schr횄쨋ter.
Returning to his native city, he entered the University of K횄쨋nigsberg to study mathematics and physics. The University of K횄쨋nigsberg had become a leading centre for mathematics during the years that Carl Jacobi worked there but he left for Berlin in June 1844 shortly before Schr횄쨋ter began his studies. However K횄쨋nigsberg still had excellent mathematicians in Friedrich Richelot and Otto Hesse who had both been students of Jacobi . Richelot was a worthy successor to Jacobi and continued to work in his spirit while Hesse continued Jacobi 's algebraic work making fundamental contributions to the algebraic treatment of analytic geometry. Another outstanding mathematician at K횄쨋nigsberg at this time was Franz Neumann who had begun working there three years before Schr횄쨋ter was born but was at the peak of his creativity in the 1840 s. Among these inspiring teachers it was Richelot who influenced Schr횄쨋ter most, introducing him to Jacobi 's work on function theory.
Schr횄쨋ter graduated from K횄쨋nigsberg in 1848 after which he spent two years doing voluntary military service in the artillery before he began to undertake research for his doctorate at the University of Berlin. At Berlin he was taught by Lejeune Dirichlet and Jakob Steiner . He attended courses by Dirichlet on number theory and on differential equations which influenced Schr횄쨋ter's teaching for the whole of his career but it was Steiner who was a major influence on Schr횄쨋ter's research. Partly this influence was through the courses Steiner delivered, but his greatest influence on Schr횄쨋ter was through personal contacts. However his doctorate was obtained from K횄쨋nigsberg on 13 July 1854 for his dissertation on the theory of elliptic functions De aequationibus modularibus 창혫혟 ( On modular equations ) which was supervised by Richelot. Immediately after graduating, Schr횄쨋ter took the state examinations to become a Gymnasium mathematics teacher but quickly decided on an academic career. He submitted his habilitation thesis 횄혵ber die Entwicklung der Potenzen der elliptischen Transcendenten Q und die Teilung dieser Functionen 창혫혟 ( On the development of the powers of the elliptical transcendentals Q and the division of these functions ) to the University of Breslau in the autumn of 1855 .
After lecturing at the University of Breslau as a dozent , he become an extraordinary professor there in 1858 . On 8 August 1860 , he married Clara Rodewald, the daughter of a lawyer from Breslau; they had four children. The chair at Breslau had been held by Eduard Kummer until 1855 when he went to Berlin and, following his departure, the chair was filled by his student Ferdinand Joachimsthal . Schr횄쨋ter was a colleague of Joachimsthal until his death in 1861 when Schr횄쨋ter succeeded to the Breslau chair. In the following year Rudolf Lipschitz was appointed as extraordinary professor at Breslau and Schr횄쨋ter, Lipschitz and M Frankenheim jointly founded a seminar in mathematics and mathematical physics ( for more details, see [ 6 ] ) . Schr횄쨋ter remained in Breslau for the rest of his life despite being offered another chair. When Alfred Clebsch left Giessen to take up the chair at G횄쨋ttingen in 1868 , Schr횄쨋ter was offered the Giessen chair but he turned it down. His last few years were badly affected by ill health and he suffered from paralysis.
Schr횄쨋ter created at Breslau the leading centre for synthetic geometry. His career in Breslau showed how strongly he had been influenced by Steiner . His first paper 횄혵ber die Erzeugnisse krummer projektivischer Gebilde 창혫혟 ( On the twisted products of projective structure ) (1857) built on work by Steiner in a paper he published in January 1856 . His next paper, however, 횄혵ber die Raumcurven dritter Klasse und dritter Ordnung 창혫혟 ( On space curves of the third-class and third-order ) (1859) built on work by Chasles in a paper he published in 1857 . In 1861 Schr횄쨋ter published 횄혵ber Modulargleichungen der elliptischen Functionen, Auszug aus einem Schreiben an Herrn L Kronecker 창혫혟 ( On modular equations of elliptic functions: extract from a letter to  Kronecker ) . After Steiner 's death in 1863 he took over the publication of his posthumous manuscripts, particularly those which were related to the application of the theory of conic sections to projective curves.  He edited his transcripts of Steiner 's lectures on synthetic geometry which he interweaved with other material by Steiner , together with his own improvements, to produce Jacob Steiner's Vorlesungen 횄쩌ber synthetische Geometrie 창혫혟 ( Jacob Steiner's lectures on synthetic geometry ) (1867) . This important work became the textbook for the synthetic geometry of conics with a second edition appearing in 1876 and a third edition in 1898 after Schr횄쨋ter's death. Next we mention two papers on third-order curves, 횄혵ber eine besondere Curve dritter Ordnung und eine einfache Erzeugungsart der allgemeinen Curven dritten Ordnung 창혫혟 ( On a special curve of the third order and a simple type of production of general curves third order ) (1872) and 횄혵ber Curven dritter Ordnung 창혫혟 ( On third-order curves ) (1873) . His next major book was Die Theorie der Oberfl횄짚chen zweiter Ordnung und der Raumkurven dritter Ordnung als Erzeugnisse projectivischer Gebilde 창혫혟 ( The theory of second order surfaces and space curves of the third order as products of projective structure ) which he published in 1880 . This work on the theory of second order surfaces and third order space curves continues Steiner 's work. In 1888 Schr횄쨋ter published a book on third-order plane curves Die Theorie der ebenen Curven dritter Ordnung, auf synthetische Weise abgeleitet 창혫혟 ( The theory of the flat curves of the third order, derived synthetically ) and in 1890 he published his major study on fourth-order space curves in Grundz횄쩌ge einer rein geometrischen Theorie der Raumcurven vierter Ordnung erster Species 창혫혟 ( General purely geometric theory of space curves of the fourth order, first species ) .
Among his doctoral students at the University of Breslau were Rudolf Sturm ( doctorate awarded in 1863 for the thesis De superficiebus tertii ordinis disquisitiones geometricae ) , Jakob Rosanes ( doctorate awarded 1865) and Moritz Pasch ( doctorate awarded in 1865 for the thesis De duarum sectionem conicarum in circulos projectione ) . Rudolf Sturm wrote the biographies [ 4 ] and [ 5 ] of Schr횄쨋ter.
Schr횄쨋ter was honoured for his contributions to synthetic geometry. He received the Steiner Prize from the Berlin Academy of Science on 6 July 1876 and, on 6 January 1881 he was elected to the Berlin Academy of Science . In [ 2 ] the opinions of Weierstrass and Kronecker on Schr횄쨋ter are discussed, these being reports on him as a candidate for election to the Academy. On 9 December 1882 he was elected a Corresponding Member of the G횄쨋ttingen Academy of Sciences .
Finally we mentions Schr횄쨋ter's interests outside mathematics. He was an excellent musician, playing the violin to a high standard in a local amateur orchestra. He was also keen on gymnastics and for twenty-five years he was the head of the Old Breslau Gymnastics Club.
